bins.name: Respect State Retail Authority
bins.desc: TAPS agrees the Commission should address large load impacts on transmission service while avoiding intrusion on state and municipal jurisdiction over retail sales, service territories, and siting. The filing cites FPA provisions and Commission precedent limiting federal reach over retail transactions.
quotes: the Commission should hold a technical conference on the topics raised in the ANOPR, with the opportunity for follow-up comments on that technical conference.

bins.name: Single Queue Reduces Gaming
bins.desc: TAPS sees merit in studying large loads and generators in a single interconnection queue to limit gaming and undue discrimination, but notes that combining the queues requires working out cost allocation details across existing region-specific tariffs.
quotes: The use of a single interconnection queue to study Qualifying Large Loads and Hybrid Facilities together with generating facilities may facilitate identifying and avoiding undue discrimination or preference. Using separate study processes for different types of facilities could create opportunities for gaming the system or raise concerns about undue discrimination.

bins.name: Deposits Deter Speculation
bins.desc: TAPS supports standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties for large load interconnections, mirroring reforms adopted for generator queues in Order 2023 to reduce speculative withdrawals and restudies.
quotes: standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties would be beneficial in the context of Qualifying Large Load interconnections. As in the context of generator interconnections, these kinds of requirements are needed to deter speculative requests that may begin the study process but then withdraw, causing delays and restudies.

bins.name: Enforce Hybrid Injection Limits
bins.desc: Studying hybrid facilities on net injection or withdrawal rights is only valid if system protection facilities actually enforce those limits. TAPS calls for mandatory protective equipment as the prerequisite for any netting-based study approach.
quotes: it is essential that they be required to have system protection facilities/mechanisms/schemes that are adequate to enforce these limits. That is the fundamental premise for studying Qualifying Large Hybrid Facilities based on their requested injection or withdrawal rights.

bins.name: System Protection Is Essential
bins.desc: TAPS insists that system protection facilities are the fundamental safeguard for enforcing hybrid facility limits, and that operational limitations may also be necessary since protective equipment can fail. Financial penalties alone are insufficient given the potential severity of grid impacts.
quotes: it is essential that they be required to have system protection facilities/mechanisms/schemes that are adequate to enforce these limits. That is the fundamental premise for studying Qualifying Large Hybrid Facilities based on their requested injection or withdrawal rights.

bins.name: Oppose Curtailment Expedited Track
bins.desc: TAPS opposes an expedited interconnection study process for curtailable large loads, arguing it would be unduly discriminatory, prone to gaming, and untethered from specific near-term resource adequacy needs. Prior Commission approvals of expedited processes were narrowly tailored and time-limited, unlike the broad approach proposed here.
quotes: Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities should not be permitted to cut the queue and receive expedited study treatment if they agree to be curtailable and dispatchable, and any standardized interconnection procedures and agreements for such facilities should be designed to not adversely affect the study of other interconnections.

Creating an expedited process for Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable raises serious concerns about undue discrimination and the potential for abuse.

These fact-specific, and time-limited, examples stand in stark contrast to an open-ended, generic expedited process for Qualifying Large Loads that agree to curtail (to an undefined extent). Such an expedited process for curtailable Qualifying Large Loads would not be narrowly tailored because it is untethered from any specific near-term need and lacks protections and limitations on the number of eligible interconnections.

bins.name: 100% Upfront Network Upgrade Cost
bins.desc: TAPS supports requiring large loads to pay 100% of assigned network upgrade costs upfront. Any crediting mechanism must be designed to prevent cost shifts to other customers and must account for the unique risk that a data center fails to sustain the demand level for which upgrades were built. The existing generator crediting model is not appropriate.
quotes: TAPS supports holding Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned. Principle 8 is key to addressing one of the most important challenges raised by large data centers and other similar loads: that, if rolled into wholesale transmission rates, the substantial costs of transmission upgrades needed to serve these unique loads gets borne by other customers
the Commission cannot achieve this goal by merely copying the existing crediting mechanisms used for different purposes in the different context of generator interconnections.
any crediting mechanism must account for the unique risks and characteristics of large data center loads to protect other customers.

bins.name: Study Process Integrity
bins.desc: TAPS supports a rigorous interconnection study process that deters speculation through deposits and penalties, and favors a single queue to reduce gaming. It opposes any expedited track that could disrupt studies for other interconnection customers.
quotes: the Commission must be proactive in addressing the huge impact that the interconnections of Qualifying Large Loads have on Commission-jurisdictional services to protect transmission customers—and, ultimately, other end-use individual and business ratepayers—from paying costs they did not cause.

The use of a single interconnection queue to study Qualifying Large Loads and Hybrid Facilities together with generating facilities may facilitate identifying and avoiding undue discrimination or preference. Using separate study processes for different types of facilities could create opportunities for gaming the system or raise concerns about undue discrimination.

standardized study deposits, readiness requirements, and withdrawal penalties would be beneficial in the context of Qualifying Large Load interconnections. As in the context of generator interconnections, these kinds of requirements are needed to deter speculative requests that may begin the study process but then withdraw, causing delays and restudies.

bins.name: Protect Other Customers From Cost Shifts
bins.desc: The central theme of the filing is that other transmission customers must not bear costs caused by large load interconnections. TAPS supports 100% upfront cost responsibility, tailored crediting that prevents stranded costs, and a Commission directive requiring retail stranded-cost protections to flow through to wholesale transmission rates.
quotes: the Commission must be proactive in addressing the huge impact that the interconnections of Qualifying Large Loads have on Commission-jurisdictional services to protect transmission customers—and, ultimately, other end-use individual and business ratepayers—from paying costs they did not cause.

a major concern with Qualifying Loads interconnections is that they may complete the interconnection process and cause major transmission upgrades to be constructed, but then fail to either attain, or maintain over a long period of time, the level of demand for which the interconnection request was studied.

TAPS supports holding Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities responsible for 100% of the network upgrades that they are assigned. Principle 8 is key to addressing one of the most important challenges raised by large data centers and other similar loads: that, if rolled into wholesale transmission rates, the substantial costs of transmission upgrades needed to serve these unique loads gets borne by other customers
the Commission cannot achieve this goal by merely copying the existing crediting mechanisms used for different purposes in the different context of generator interconnections.
any crediting mechanism must account for the unique risks and characteristics of large data center loads to protect other customers.

the Commission should find that it is unjust, unreasonable, and unduly discriminatory for a public utility to charge rates that fail to reflect the impact of ratepayer protections imposed by retail arrangements with Qualifying Large Loads.

the Commission should ensure that other customers are protected from harm resulting from large data center load interconnections already being studied, as well as earlier data center-driven network upgrades that are subject to ratepayer protection provisions by contract or retail tariff.

bins.name: Co-located Generation Reliability Study
bins.desc: TAPS supports an SSR/RMR-type study before any generator partially suspends to serve a co-located large load, and argues the study must include resource adequacy and capacity market analysis. Results should be made public to allow regulators and stakeholders to assess impacts.
quotes: TAPS agrees that there should be an SSR/RMR-type study for existing generating facilities that seek to enter a partial suspension to service a Qualifying Large Load at the same location. Such a study is important to ensure reliability of the transmission system.

the unique size and accelerated timing of new large data center loads, coupled with existing resource adequacy challenges (given retirements of generation and generation interconnection queue backlogs creating barriers to their timely replacement), make it vital to understand the resource adequacy and capacity market implications of taking generation that now serves regional load and instead sequestering it to exclusively serve a co-located large data center load.

bins.name: Request Technical Conference First
bins.desc: TAPS asks the Commission to hold a technical conference with follow-up comment opportunity before issuing a rulemaking, citing the complexity of large load issues, regional variation, and fast-moving developments.
quotes: the Commission should hold a technical conference on the topics raised in the ANOPR, with the opportunity for follow-up comments on that technical conference.

bins.name: Stranded Cost Risk From Demand Shortfall
bins.desc: TAPS identifies a specific risk that deposits and penalties do not address: a load that completes interconnection and triggers expensive upgrades but then fails to sustain the planned demand level, leaving other customers with stranded transmission costs.
quotes: a major concern with Qualifying Loads interconnections is that they may complete the interconnection process and cause major transmission upgrades to be constructed, but then fail to either attain, or maintain over a long period of time, the level of demand for which the interconnection request was studied.

bins.name: No Queue Jump for Curtailable Loads
bins.desc: TAPS argues curtailability should be rewarded through smaller network upgrade cost assignments and market participation, not through queue-jumping. An open-ended expedited track for curtailable loads lacks the narrow tailoring and time limits that FERC has required when approving similar mechanisms for generators in MISO and SPP.
quotes: Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities should not be permitted to cut the queue and receive expedited study treatment if they agree to be curtailable and dispatchable, and any standardized interconnection procedures and agreements for such facilities should be designed to not adversely affect the study of other interconnections.

Creating an expedited process for Qualifying Large Load and Hybrid Facilities that agree to be curtailable and dispatchable raises serious concerns about undue discrimination and the potential for abuse.

These fact-specific, and time-limited, examples stand in stark contrast to an open-ended, generic expedited process for Qualifying Large Loads that agree to curtail (to an undefined extent). Such an expedited process for curtailable Qualifying Large Loads would not be narrowly tailored because it is untethered from any specific near-term need and lacks protections and limitations on the number of eligible interconnections.

bins.name: Crediting Must Fit Large Load Risk
bins.desc: TAPS warns that copying the generator interconnection crediting mechanism would fail to protect other customers from stranded costs because that mechanism was designed as a financing tool, not a cost-causation safeguard. Any credit offset must be tailored to the risk that a data center does not sustain expected demand.
quotes: the Commission cannot achieve this goal by merely copying the existing crediting mechanisms used for different purposes in the different context of generator interconnections.
any crediting mechanism must account for the unique risks and characteristics of large data center loads to protect other customers.

bins.name: Co-location Resource Adequacy Transparency
bins.desc: TAPS urges that the SSR/RMR study for co-located arrangements include resource adequacy and capacity market analysis, and that results be made public so regulators and stakeholders can evaluate whether additional steps are needed to maintain affordability.
quotes: the unique size and accelerated timing of new large data center loads, coupled with existing resource adequacy challenges (given retirements of generation and generation interconnection queue backlogs creating barriers to their timely replacement), make it vital to understand the resource adequacy and capacity market implications of taking generation that now serves regional load and instead sequestering it to exclusively serve a co-located large data center load.

bins.name: Retail Protections Must Flow to Wholesale Rates
bins.desc: TAPS argues that retail stranded-cost protections in data center tariffs or contracts must be reflected in Commission-jurisdictional wholesale transmission rates. Failing to do so is unjust and discriminatory, and the Commission has the authority and obligation to require it.
quotes: the Commission should find that it is unjust, unreasonable, and unduly discriminatory for a public utility to charge rates that fail to reflect the impact of ratepayer protections imposed by retail arrangements with Qualifying Large Loads.

bins.name: Protect Customers in Current Queue Now
bins.desc: TAPS insists protections must apply to large load interconnections already under study and earlier network upgrades already governed by retail stranded-cost provisions, not only future loads covered by a new standard rule.
quotes: the Commission should ensure that other customers are protected from harm resulting from large data center load interconnections already being studied, as well as earlier data center-driven network upgrades that are subject to ratepayer protection provisions by contract or retail tariff.
